title: Standard Feed-Forward Network
description: The default dense feed-forward layer that expands, activates, and projects each token state after attention.
openingSummary: A standard feed-forward network, often shortened to standard FFN, is the default dense layer after attention in a transformer block, using one shared expand-activate-project path for every token.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A standard feed-forward network, often shortened to standard FFN, is the default dense feed-forward block used in most transformer layers. After attention updates a token state, the layer sends that state through one wide linear layer, applies a pointwise activation, and projects it back to the model width.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: The standard feed-forward network optimizes for a simple, uniform dense path after attention. Every token uses the same parameters and the same full hidden width, which keeps the block easy to reason about and easy to compare against later variants.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state enters one shared dense block, expands into a wider hidden width, passes through an activation, and then projects back to the model width. No router chooses between experts, and no separate gate branch decides which hidden features stay active.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The dense feed-forward formula below is the baseline for the whole family. It shows the single shared expand, activate, and project path that many later variants start from.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with other feed-forward modules, the standard feed-forward network keeps the most direct dense path. The main tradeoff is that every token activates the full hidden block every time.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Many early and still-common transformer stacks use the standard dense feed-forward network as the default post-attention module.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: The standard feed-forward network activates the whole hidden path for every token, so it cannot add expert capacity sparsely the way a mixture-of-experts layer can. It also lacks the extra gating flexibility that later dense variants such as the swish gated linear unit introduce.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Even when a paper introduces a new feed-forward variant, the standard feed-forward network remains the reference point that explains what changed. It is the baseline shape many readers still need first.

computeFlow.alt: Standard feed-forward network graph showing one dense expand, activate, and project path for each token

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared feed-forward block
standard.mainTradeoff: Simple and predictable, but every token pays for the full block

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch multiplied by a SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainTradeoff: More expressive, but the block is less minimal than the dense baseline

moe.pathShape: Router chooses a few expert layers, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainTradeoff: Much more capacity, but routing and load balancing add complexity

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection into the wide hidden layer.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation inside the hidden layer.

swigluSchema.label: Swish gated linear unit comparison target

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

wv.definition: Projection that builds the value branch.

wg.definition: Projection that builds the gate branch.

wo.definition: Projection back to model width after gating.

odot.definition: Elementwise multiplication between the value and gate branches.

noRouterNote.label: No router and no expert choice: every token uses this same single hidden layer

feedForwardDescription.label: Take data, multiply by matrix, pass through activation, return

denseNote.label: Every token uses one shared expand -> hidden layer -> activate -> project path
